title: Friction & rolling
summary: Dry (Coulomb) friction in stick vs slip regimes, the friction cone ($f=\tan\varphi$), and the standard assume-stick-then-check-the-cap method. Covers rolling resistance ($F=Nu/r$), pin/bearing friction (friction circle, V-bearing $f'=f/\sin\alpha$), gyroscopic effects ($M=J_p\omega\Omega$ and rotor unbalance), and vehicle traction/overturning. Ten fully worked exam problems (23-32) on rollers, plates, inclines, bearings, car start and wheelbarrow, each verified against the answer key.
objectives: Distinguish static (stick, $f_a$) from kinetic (slip, $f$) friction and apply the friction cone $f=\tan\varphi$
Use the assume-stick / check-the-cap method to decide whether a body slides, sticks, rolls or tips
Compute rolling resistance $F=Nu/r$ and bearing/pin friction (friction circle, V-bearing $f'=f/\sin\alpha$)
Evaluate gyroscopic moments $M=J_p\omega\Omega$ and balance an unbalanced (misaligned) rotor
Analyse vehicle start with load transfer and the traction limit $T\le f_aN$, and find overturning limits
Solve the standard friction/rolling exam problems (rollers, plates, inclines, brakes-of-motion) end to end

blocks.text: Dry (Coulomb) friction is the tangential force at a contact that opposes (or tends to oppose) relative sliding. Two regimes matter: **static/stick** (no sliding) and **dynamic/kinetic/slip** (sliding). The normal force $N$ presses the surfaces together; friction is proportional to it.

$f_a$ (static/adherence) governs the **threshold** of motion; $f$ (kinetic/slip) governs the force **while sliding**. Usually $f_a > f$. While stuck, friction is whatever the equilibrium needs, up to the cap $f_a N$. Once it slips, friction snaps to the fixed value $fN$ opposing the velocity.

The **friction cone** has half-angle $\varphi=\arctan f$ about the normal. The total contact reaction (normal + friction) stays inside the cone while sticking and lies **on** the cone surface while sliding. This is the geometric way to spot the slip limit.

The standard decision tree: **assume stick**, solve statics for the required friction $T$, then check $T\le f_a N$. If it violates the cap, the body **slides**: set $T=fN$ opposing the motion and re-solve the dynamics.

A wheel/roller of radius $r$ rolling without slip still meets resistance because the normal pressure shifts forward by a small distance $u$ (the rolling-friction parameter, in mm). The equivalent horizontal resistive force at the axle is:

blocks.tex: F_{\text{roll}} = N\,\frac{u}{r}\qquad\Big(\text{a roller between plate and floor: } F = N\,\frac{u_1+u_2}{d}\Big)

blocks.text: A loaded pin of radius $r_p$ with friction angle $\varphi$ (or coefficient $f$) makes the reaction line tangent to a **friction circle** of radius $\rho=r_p\sin\varphi\approx r_p f$. The friction moment is $M=N\,r_p\sin\varphi$. For a **V-bearing** of semi-angle $\alpha$ the effective coefficient grows: $f'=f/\sin\alpha$, so the friction torque is $C=f'\,W\,r=\dfrac{f\,W\,r}{\sin\alpha}$.
A spinning rotor (angular momentum $H=J_p\,\omega$ along its polar axis) that is forced to **precess** at rate $\Omega$ reacts with a gyroscopic moment perpendicular to both:

blocks.tex: M_{\text{gyro}} = J_p\,\omega\,\Omega \qquad(\Omega = V/R \text{ for a vehicle in a curve of radius } R)

blocks.text: For a **misaligned rotor** (polar axis at small angle $\alpha$ to the spin axis), the unbalance couple is $M=(J_t-J_p)\,\omega^2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha$. This rotating couple loads the bearings; balance it with two small masses giving an equal, opposite couple: $m\,\omega^2 r\,\ell = M$.

Traction at a driven wheel is capped by stick: $T\le f_a N$. During acceleration, load transfers to the rear (driving) axle, which **raises** $N_{\text{rear}}$ and the available grip. Solve longitudinal Newton, vertical equilibrium, and a moment equation about the CG (with the inertia force $Ma$ at height $z_G$). For a body pushed high up, check **overturning**: the normal force shifts toward the front edge; if it would pass the edge, the body tips ($\ddot\theta\ne0$, $\ddot y\ne0$).

steps.label: 1 Free-body
steps.text: Draw every contact: normal $N$, friction $T$, weight, applied forces, and inertia terms $ma$, $J\ddot\theta$ for dynamics.

steps.label: 2 Assume stick
steps.text: Write equilibrium/Newton with $T$ as an unknown $\le f_a N$. For rolling, add the no-slip link $a=\alpha r$ and the friction is also unknown.

steps.label: 3 Check the cap
steps.text: Compute required $T$ and compare to $f_a N$. If $T\le f_aN$ it sticks/rolls; otherwise it slides.

steps.label: 4 If slipping
steps.text: Set $T=fN$ opposing the actual relative velocity, drop the no-slip constraint, and re-solve for $a$ and $\alpha$ independently.

steps.label: 5 Moments for N-position / tipping
steps.text: Take moments about the CG (or contact edge) to locate $N$ or to find the tipping limit $h_{\max}$ (N at the edge).

blocks.text: Classic mistakes: (1) using $f_a$ instead of $f$ once the body is **moving** (kinetic friction governs accelerations); (2) forgetting that a rolling body needs friction to provide angular acceleration — friction is an unknown, not $fN$, until you prove it slips; (3) sign of the gyroscopic moment — it can either increase or decrease a lean angle depending on spin/travel sense; (4) on inclines, $N=mg\cos\beta$, not $mg$.

question.prompt: A block rests on a plane with $f_a=0.3$. The applied tangential force needs a friction reaction of $T=0.25\,N$ to stay in equilibrium. What happens?

options.content: It stays stuck; friction is exactly $0.25N$

question.explanation: Required friction $0.25N \le f_aN=0.3N$, so the block sticks and friction supplies exactly what equilibrium demands ($0.25N$), not the maximum. Friction only equals $fN$ when actually sliding.

question.prompt: A homogeneous cylinder ($J=\tfrac12 mr^2$) released on an incline rolls without slipping. Its centre acceleration is:
question.explanation: Newton: $mg\sin\beta-T=ma$; rotation: $Tr=\tfrac12 mr^2\,(a/r)\Rightarrow T=\tfrac12 ma$. Substituting gives $g\sin\beta=\tfrac32 a$, so $a=\tfrac{2}{3}g\sin\beta$ (provided the required $T\le f_aN$).
